meta.data_vintage: 2023 Census ACS 5-Year, BLS 2024, Alabama state reports 2023-2024

population_demographics.source: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts: Montgomery city, Alabama

language_access.note: 6% (~11,880 residents) have a language access need. Small by national standards but non-trivial at household scale.
language_access.source: Census ACS 2023 via web search aggregation

digital_divide.note_on_92pct: Includes mobile-data-only plans. Does NOT imply reliable broadband for all uses.
digital_divide.digital_skills_gap: Many low-income households not confident in basic digital skills
digital_divide.device_access_gap: Low-income, senior, and disabled households lag significantly in device ownership
digital_divide.acp_underutilization: Federal Affordable Connectivity Program subsidies under-utilized in Montgomery
digital_divide.senior_digital_lag: Senior households lag behind in accessing medical and government services digitally
digital_divide.source: Montgomery County Broadband Profile July 2023 (ADECA + Census); Community Action Association Alabama

senior_population.note: 30,892 seniors — 15.57% of population — many with digital access and navigation barriers

disability_population.note: Montgomery County (broader than city). City disability rate likely similar. Alabama disability rate higher than national average.
disability_population.alabama_disability_context: Alabama has among the highest disability rates in the Southeast

crime_and_safety.crime_rate_context: One of the highest in America vs all community sizes

geographic_disparity.northwest_per_capita_rate: 1 in 9 chance of being a crime victim
geographic_disparity.note: Northwest (higher poverty, more Black residents) has far higher per-capita crime rate

crime_and_safety.poverty_crime_link: 19.7% poverty overall; 30.8% Black poverty rate — strong geographic overlap with high-crime zones

park_equity_access.historical_context: Montgomery parks directly reflect history of segregation. City closed all parks in 1958 rather than desegregate.
park_equity_access.current_state: Poor maintenance documented in predominantly Black neighbourhood parks (Fairview Environmental Park)
park_equity_access.segregation_legacy: I-65/I-85 construction displaced 1,700+ families (~75% Black) in 1960s-70s
park_equity_access.federal_investment: $36M federal Reconnecting Communities and Neighbourhoods Initiative grant received 2024
park_equity_access.envision_montgomery_2040: City's comprehensive plan calls for park upgrades — community prioritisation needed
park_equity_access.source: The Conversation (July 2024), UPI (2024), Segregation by Design, Binita Mahato academic study (2024)

alabama_medicaid.expansion_status: NOT expanded — most stringent eligibility in the US (tied with Texas)

snap_utilization.note: SNAP requires work registration for ages 16-60 in Montgomery County — barrier to enrollment

wic.auto_eligible_if: Already on SNAP, Medicaid, or TANF

benefits_navigation_gap.core_finding: Benefits navigation is a documented, quantifiable gap — 15,578 people in Montgomery County alone lost Medicaid in one 12-month period. The root cause is bureaucratic complexity, not ineligibility.
benefits_navigation_gap.source: Alabama Decatur Daily 2024, Alabama Arise, CBPP SNAP Factsheet Alabama, ADPH WIC

311_service.note: No AI chatbot. Basic 311 service only — not integrated with benefits, permits, or civic navigation.

city_services_landscape.budget_sources: Sales/use taxes, ad valorem taxes, business licenses
city_services_landscape.community_development: Community Development Division administers federal, state, and local funds for low-to-moderate income individuals
city_services_landscape.source: City of Montgomery website, Montgomery Open Data Portal

gap_analysis.core_insight: Montgomery has a large, fragmented population of vulnerable residents — elderly (15.57%), disabled (8.7%+), low-income (19.7%), non-English speaking (6%) — who are ENTITLED to government services and benefits but FAIL to navigate the complex, siloed, paper-heavy system to claim them. The result: $M in unclaimed benefits annually, 15K+ people losing Medicaid coverage they were likely still eligible for, and no single touchpoint for civic navigation.
gap_analysis.why_no_spreadsheet: Eligibility rules change. Benefits interact. Agencies are different. Language is a barrier. A RAG-powered LLM can explain eligibility, translate, summarize, and guide — a spreadsheet cannot.
why_ai_specifically: Benefits eligibility requires natural language reasoning across 10+ overlapping rule sets
Citizens describe their situations in natural language — LLMs understand; forms do not
Multilingual support: real-time Spanish/English translation with context preservation
RAG over fragmented city/county/state/federal policy documents enables single-interface access
Seniors and low-literacy users benefit from conversational guidance over web navigation

persona.name: Dorothy
persona.household: Lives alone; one adult child 30 min away
persona.digital_access: Has a smartphone, no laptop. Not confident online.
persona.current_situation: Lost Medicaid coverage in the 2023 unwinding. Has not re-enrolled. Doesn't know she may still qualify.
potentially_eligible_for: SNAP food assistance (~$200/mo at her income level)
Prescription drug coverage (Medicare Extra Help / LIS)
pain_points: Calls the city and gets transferred 3-4 times without resolution
Different agencies for different programs — doesn't know where to start
Application forms are long and confusing — she can start but often abandons
Afraid of making a mistake that disqualifies her
No one told her Medicaid unwinding happened or how to re-enroll
what_she_has: A smartphone with basic browsing capability
A clear need — she is below 150% FPL
Motivation: avoiding debt, staying in her home
persona.why_data_confirmed: 30,892 seniors in Montgomery (15.57%). 30.8% Black poverty rate. 15,578 Montgomery County residents lost Medicaid in 2023-2024 unwinding. This persona is not invented — she is a statistical reality at scale.

problem_statement: In Montgomery, AL — where 19.7% of residents live in poverty, 15,578 residents lost Medicaid coverage in a single 12-month period, 30,892 elderly residents struggle with digital navigation, 11,880 residents have limited English proficiency, and the city's benefits, permits, and services are split across 10+ agencies with no unified entry point — there is no single conversational tool that takes a resident's situation, tells them exactly which services they qualify for, explains eligibility in plain language, and guides them through the first step.
verdict_rationale: Clear LLM problem (language + reasoning + fragmented docs). Large addressable population (ALL Montgomery residents, especially 19.7% in poverty, 15.57% seniors, 6% non-English). No existing solution locally. Data sufficient. Demo is simple and compelling — just a chatbot conversation. Differentiated from generic job board / workforce solutions. Complements the Workforce Growth track architecture.

civic_access_advantages: Broader user base — ALL residents, not just working-age
Simpler core architecture — RAG + LLM, less ML complexity
More naturally LLM-native (language navigation vs ML ranking)
Easier live demo (conversation is immediately intuitive to judges)
Strong emotional resonance (Dorothy losing Medicaid is a compelling story)
workforce_growth_advantages: Richer, more structured ML pipeline (skill matching, gap scoring)
Bright Data bonus points stronger use case (live job scraping)
vs_workforce_growth.recommendation: If solo or 1 teammate: pick Workforce Growth (higher technical ceiling). If 2+ teammates: dual track is feasible — shared ETL and RAG layer, two agents on one platform.
